narrative:
Short impact summary

Published in 2023 by Peter Lang Verlag, Christian Democracy and Labour after World War II by Andrea Maria Locatelli and Cecilia Maria Bravi is an early-stage scholarly contribution situating postwar Christian democratic politics within the broader history of labour and social policy in Europe. The book has established a retail and reader-discovery presence through major commercial platforms, and its thematic alignment with decent work and economic growth connects it to live policy conversations, though formal academic citation has not yet accumulated.

Full narrative

The book enters a well-populated but persistently contested historiographical space: the intersection of Christian democracy, organised labour, and the reconstruction of European civil society after 1945. By focusing on this nexus, Locatelli and Bravi address questions that remain politically resonant—how confessional political traditions shaped welfare states, labour relations, and the boundaries of democratic governance in the postwar decades. The conceptual terrain mapped by the work spans democracy, civil society, political economy, and social change, reflecting the genuinely interdisciplinary character of the inquiry.

The volume appears as part of the long-running Peter Lang series Travail et Société / Work and Society, whose catalogue includes titles examining European trade union dynamics, pension reform, austerity policy, and workplace diversity. Placement in this series situates the book within a community of scholars engaged with the structural and institutional dimensions of work across European contexts, lending it intellectual companionship even at an early point in its citation life. Series peers such as Jeremy Waddington's study of trade unions in the European Union and David Natali's analysis of pension reform provide a sense of the comparative, policy-oriented register the series cultivates.

From an impact-evidence standpoint, the book's most tangible footprint at this stage is commercial and discovery-oriented: its availability through major bookselling and reader platforms indicates active distribution and at least nascent reader interest beyond specialist library acquisition. Library holdings, catalogued under the subjects of European history and social policy, confirm that the work is entering institutional collections, a necessary precondition for coursework adoption and deeper scholarly engagement. Its alignment with UN Sustainable Development Goal 8—Decent Work and Economic Growth—underscores that the historical questions it addresses carry direct relevance for contemporary policy analysts and practitioners thinking about the long-run determinants of labour rights and economic inclusion.

As a 2023 publication, the absence of recorded citations reflects the normal temporal lag of the scholarly review and response cycle rather than any signal of limited relevance. The book's current citation percentile places it in the early distribution of its cohort, consistent with a work that has not yet had opportunity to accumulate scholarly responses. The historical and political-science communities it addresses typically engage on timescales of one to several years, and the substantive questions the volume raises—about the ideological architecture of postwar labour policy and the role of Christian democratic movements in shaping it—are durable ones. Continued monitoring of citation activity, library adoption, and potential course syllabi integration will provide a more complete picture of the book's scholarly reach in the years ahead.
